# MedLit

> Free AI-powered scientific literature analyzer for researchers, clinicians, and medical students. Paste any PubMed, DOI, or journal URL and get a multi-dimensional critical appraisal report in seconds.

MedLit automates evidence-based critical appraisal of scientific papers using established medical research frameworks. It is free to use (bring your own Anthropic Claude API key) and requires no account.

## What MedLit does

- Scores any medical or scientific paper across 8 dimensions using AI
- Accepts PubMed, DOI (doi.org), Nature, NEJM, Lancet, BMJ, JAMA, bioRxiv, medRxiv, and most major journal URLs
- Returns results in ~30–60 seconds using Claude (Sonnet or Opus) at temperature 0.2
- Exports to plain text, Markdown, or BibTeX

## Scoring dimensions

| Dimension | Scale | Framework |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy Score | 1–10 | Internal validity, CEBM, pre-registration |
| Bias Risk Rating | 1–10 | Cochrane RoB 2, ROBINS-I, Newcastle-Ottawa Scale |
| Methods Quality Score | 1–10 | CONSORT, STROBE, PRISMA, EQUATOR |
| Reference Index Check | PASS / WARN / FAIL | Citation-claim mapping, self-citation rate |
| Study Design | Level 1–5 | Oxford CEBM Levels of Evidence |
| Conflict of Interest | FLAG / CLEAR / DECLARED | ICMJE standards |
| Limitations | List | Enumerated from paper text |
| Plain-Language Summary | 6-part | NLM Health Literacy Guidelines |

## Standards and frameworks used

- Oxford Centre for Evidence-Based Medicine (CEBM) Levels of Evidence
- Cochrane Risk of Bias 2 (RoB 2) — for randomized trials
- ROBINS-I — for non-randomized studies
- Newcastle-Ottawa Scale — for observational studies
- CONSORT 2010 — for randomized controlled trial reporting
- STROBE — for observational study reporting
- PRISMA 2020 — for systematic reviews and meta-analyses
- EQUATOR Network reporting standards
- ICMJE conflict-of-interest guidelines
- NLM Health Literacy Guidelines

## Who it is for

- Medical students learning critical appraisal
- Residents and clinicians reviewing evidence for clinical decisions
- Researchers evaluating related literature
- Academics teaching evidence-based medicine
- Science journalists and policy analysts assessing study quality
- Anyone who needs to quickly evaluate whether a paper is trustworthy

## Key pages

- [Home — analyze a paper](https://medlit.app/)
- [How MedLit works (Methodology)](https://medlit.app/methodology)
- [Frequently Asked Questions](https://medlit.app/faq)
